nodes.name: Plays & Playwrights
nodes.description: Dramatic works and the writers who created them

items.prompt: Which 1949 Arthur Miller play follows aging Brooklyn salesman Willy Loman as he unravels under the weight of failed ambitions for himself and his sons, Biff and Happy?
items.explanation:
Opened February 10, 1949 at the Morosco Theatre; written by Arthur Miller, directed by Elia Kazan
Won the 1949 Pulitzer Prize for Drama, the Tony Award for Best Play, and the New York Drama Critics' Circle Award
Original production ran 742 performances; Lee J. Cobb originated the role of Willy Loman
Willy Loman is a traveling salesman whose delusions about success and the American Dream drive him to suicide
Miller wrote the first act in a single day at a Connecticut cabin he had just built
The 1951 film starred Fredric March; later revivals starred Dustin Hoffman (1984) and Denzel Washington (2012)
Often cited alongside *The Crucible* as Miller's greatest work; it remains one of the most-produced American plays worldwide
items.cardFront: Arthur Miller, 1949 — Brooklyn salesman, failed American Dream

items.prompt: Which 1947 Tennessee Williams play depicts a fading Southern belle who moves in with her sister Stella and brutish brother-in-law Stanley Kowalski in the French Quarter of New Orleans?
items.explanation:
Opened December 3, 1947 at the Ethel Barrymore Theatre; directed by Elia Kazan
Won the 1948 Pulitzer Prize for Drama; original run of 855 performances
Marlon Brando starred as Stanley Kowalski at age 23, launching him to stardom; Jessica Tandy played Blanche DuBois and won the Tony for Best Actress
The 1951 film retained Brando, Kim Hunter, and Karl Malden but replaced Tandy with Vivien Leigh, who won the Oscar
Blanche's line "I have always depended on the kindness of strangers" is one of the most quoted in American drama
Brando's raw, animalistic performance as Stanley redefined stage acting and influenced Method acting's rise in Hollywood
The opening-night curtain call lasted 30 minutes; the audience sat in stunned silence before erupting
items.cardFront: Tennessee Williams, 1947 — Blanche DuBois, New Orleans, Stanley Kowalski

items.prompt: Which 1953 Arthur Miller play dramatizes the 1692 Salem witch trials as an allegory for McCarthyism, centering on farmer John Proctor's refusal to make a false confession?
items.explanation:
Opened January 22, 1953 at the Martin Beck Theatre; directed by Jed Harris
Won the 1953 Tony Award for Best Play despite mixed initial reviews
Miller wrote it as a direct allegory for the anti-Communist hysteria of Senator Joseph McCarthy's congressional hearings
Miller himself was called before the House Un-American Activities Committee in 1956 and refused to name names
Original run was only 197 performances; the play gained stature over time and is now one of the most-performed American plays
The 1996 film starred Daniel Day-Lewis as Proctor and Winona Ryder as Abigail Williams, with a screenplay by Miller
Based on actual court records from Salem, though Miller altered ages and combined characters for dramatic effect
items.cardFront: Arthur Miller, 1953 — Salem witch trials, McCarthyism allegory

items.prompt: Which absurdist tragicomedy by Samuel Beckett features two tramps, Vladimir and Estragon, who spend the entire play on a country road expecting someone who never arrives?
items.explanation:
World premiere January 5, 1953 at the Théâtre de Babylone in Paris; Broadway premiere April 19, 1956 with Bert Lahr and E.G. Marshall
Originally written in French as *En attendant Godot* (1948–1949); Beckett translated it into English himself
The definitive work of the Theatre of the Absurd; nothing happens—twice (one critic's famous summary of its two acts)
Beckett won the 1969 Nobel Prize in Literature; he called his work a "tragicomedy in two acts"
Beckett never confirmed who or what Godot represents; he reportedly said, "If I knew, I would have said so in the play"
The first American production (1956) starred Bert Lahr, best known as the Cowardly Lion in *The Wizard of Oz*
Some audience members walked out at the Paris premiere; the play divided critics but became one of the most influential works of 20th-century theatre
items.cardFront: Samuel Beckett, 1953 — Vladimir and Estragon, Theatre of the Absurd

items.prompt: Which autobiographical Eugene O'Neill play, set over a single day in a Connecticut summer home, follows the morphine-addicted mother and alcoholic father of the Tyrone family as they confront guilt, blame, and fog?
items.explanation:
Written 1939–1941; premiered February 2, 1956 at the Royal Dramatic Theatre in Stockholm, then Broadway in November 1956
Awarded the 1957 Pulitzer Prize for Drama posthumously—O'Neill had died in 1953
O'Neill forbade publication or production during his lifetime; his widow Carlotta overrode his wishes and gave it to Yale University Press in 1956
Openly autobiographical: the Tyrone family maps to O'Neill's own—James Sr. (his actor father), Mary (morphine-addicted mother), Jamie and Edmund (his brother and himself)
O'Neill dedicated the manuscript to Carlotta: "with tears and blood... this play of old sorrow, written in tears and blood"
Broadway production starred Fredric March and Florence Eldridge; won the Tony Award for Best Play
Widely considered O'Neill's masterpiece and one of the greatest American plays ever written
items.cardFront: Eugene O'Neill, written 1941/premiered 1956 — autobiographical, Tyrone family

items.prompt: Which 1962 Edward Albee play unfolds over one alcohol-fueled night as a middle-aged history professor and his wife psychologically destroy a younger couple they have invited over after a faculty party?
items.explanation:
Opened October 13, 1962 at the Billy Rose Theatre; original cast led by Uta Hagen (Martha) and Arthur Hill (George)
Won the 1963 Tony Award for Best Play and the New York Drama Critics' Circle Award
The Pulitzer Prize drama jury selected it for the 1963 prize, but Columbia University trustees vetoed the choice due to profanity and sexual content—no Pulitzer for Drama was awarded that year
The 1966 film starred Elizabeth Taylor and Richard Burton; Taylor won the Oscar for Best Actress
Albee's title is a pun on "Who's Afraid of the Big Bad Wolf?"—it was scrawled on a bar's mirror, which inspired him
The play's structure follows three "games" the couple plays: "Humiliate the Host," "Get the Guests," and "Bringing Up Baby"
Albee went on to win Pulitzer Prizes for *A Delicate Balance* (1967), *Seascape* (1975), and *Three Tall Women* (1994)
items.cardFront: Edward Albee, 1962 — George and Martha, faculty party, psychological warfare

items.prompt: Which two-part Tony Kushner epic, subtitled 'A Gay Fantasia on National Themes,' interweaves the AIDS crisis, Mormonism, McCarthyism, and Roy Cohn's closeted life in 1980s New York?
items.explanation:
Part One (*Millennium Approaches*) premiered on Broadway May 4, 1993; Part Two (*Perestroika*) followed November 23, 1993
World premiere of Part One: Eureka Theatre Company, San Francisco, May 1991
Both parts won the Tony Award for Best Play (1993 and 1994)—the only work to win the award twice
*Millennium Approaches* won the 1993 Pulitzer Prize for Drama
Subtitled "A Gay Fantasia on National Themes"; combines realism, fantasy, and political satire across seven hours of theatre
HBO adapted both parts as a 2003 miniseries starring Al Pacino as Roy Cohn and Meryl Streep in multiple roles; it won 11 Emmy Awards
Roy Cohn, the real-life McCarthy-era lawyer who died of AIDS in 1986 while denying his homosexuality, is the play's central antagonist
items.cardFront: Tony Kushner, 1991/1993 — AIDS crisis, Roy Cohn, two-part epic

items.prompt: Which 1987 August Wilson play, set in 1950s Pittsburgh, follows a former Negro Leagues baseball player now working as a garbage collector who builds barriers—literal and figurative—around his family?
items.explanation:
Opened March 26, 1987 at the 46th Street Theatre; directed by Lloyd Richards, starring James Earl Jones as Troy Maxson
Won the 1987 Pulitzer Prize for Drama and the Tony Award for Best Play; Jones won Best Actor, Mary Alice won Best Featured Actress
Sixth play in Wilson's ten-play Pittsburgh Cycle, which chronicles the African-American experience in each decade of the 20th century
Troy Maxson was too old for Major League Baseball when it integrated in 1947; his bitterness poisons his relationship with son Cory
Denzel Washington directed and starred in the 2016 film adaptation; Viola Davis won the Oscar for Best Supporting Actress, reprising her Tony-winning role as Rose
Wilson wrote all ten Pittsburgh Cycle plays before his death from liver cancer in 2005 at age 60
The title refers both to the fence Troy builds in his backyard and the metaphorical barriers of race, family, and ambition
items.cardFront: August Wilson, 1987 — Pittsburgh Cycle, former Negro Leagues player

items.prompt: Which 1959 play by Lorraine Hansberry follows the Younger family on Chicago's South Side as they debate how to spend a $10,000 life-insurance check, with its title drawn from a Langston Hughes poem?
items.explanation:
Opened March 11, 1959 at the Ethel Barrymore Theatre; directed by Lloyd Richards, starring Sidney Poitier as Walter Lee Younger
First play by a Black woman produced on Broadway; Hansberry was 29 years old at the premiere
Lloyd Richards was also the first Black director of a Broadway play
Title comes from Langston Hughes's poem "Harlem" (1951): "What happens to a dream deferred? / Does it dry up / like a raisin in the sun?"
Won the New York Drama Critics' Circle Award for Best Play, beating out works by Tennessee Williams and Eugene O'Neill
Original run of 530 performances; the cast included Ruby Dee, Claudia McNeil, and a young Louis Gossett Jr.
Hansberry died of pancreatic cancer in 1965 at age 34; her ex-husband Robert Nemiroff managed her literary estate and adapted her writings into the musical *Raisin* (1973)
items.cardFront: Lorraine Hansberry, 1959 — Younger family, Chicago South Side, Langston Hughes poem

items.prompt: Which 1938 Thornton Wilder play, performed with almost no scenery or props, uses a Stage Manager narrator to guide the audience through daily life, love, and death in the fictional New Hampshire village of Grover's Corners?
items.explanation:
Opened February 4, 1938 at Henry Miller's Theatre in New York; directed and produced by Jed Harris
Won the 1938 Pulitzer Prize for Drama; ran over 300 performances at the Morosco Theatre
Revolutionized American theatre with its bare-stage approach: no scenery, minimal props, actors mime daily activities
The Stage Manager breaks the fourth wall, narrating directly to the audience and even playing minor roles
Act III depicts Emily Webb returning from the dead to relive her 12th birthday—one of the most emotionally devastating scenes in American drama
Wilder himself played the Stage Manager for two weeks during the original Broadway run
Second of Wilder's three Pulitzer Prizes (the others: the novel *The Bridge of San Luis Rey* in 1928, and the play *The Skin of Our Teeth* in 1943)
items.cardFront: Thornton Wilder, 1938 — Grover's Corners, bare stage, Stage Manager narrator

items.prompt: Which Tennessee Williams 'memory play,' set in a cramped St. Louis apartment, follows a domineering mother's faded Southern hopes, her painfully shy daughter's collection of tiny animal figurines, and a son who longs to escape?
items.explanation:
Premiered in Chicago December 26, 1944; opened on Broadway March 31, 1945 at the Playhouse Theatre
Williams's first major success; won the New York Drama Critics' Circle Award for Best American Play
Laurette Taylor's performance as Amanda Wingfield is considered one of the greatest in Broadway history; she died the following year
Williams called it a "memory play"—the narrator Tom recounts events from his past, and the staging is deliberately non-realistic
Openly autobiographical: Tom is Williams himself, Laura is modeled on his sister Rose (who was lobotomized in 1943), and Amanda on their mother
The play nearly closed in Chicago after poor ticket sales; critics Claudia Cassidy and Ashton Stevens championed it and saved the run
The fragile glass unicorn that Laura treasures is the play's central symbol—when the Gentleman Caller accidentally breaks its horn, it becomes "just like all the other horses"
items.cardFront: Tennessee Williams, 1944 — memory play, St. Louis, glass figurines

items.prompt: Which 1984 David Mamet play depicts a group of desperate Chicago real-estate salesmen resorting to manipulation, bribery, and theft to avoid being fired in a ruthless sales competition?
items.explanation:
World premiere September 21, 1983 at the National Theatre in London; Broadway premiere March 25, 1984 at the John Golden Theatre
Won the 1984 Pulitzer Prize for Drama; Joe Mantegna won the Tony for Best Featured Actor
The title combines the names of fictional real-estate developments the salesmen are hawking
The 1992 film added Alec Baldwin's character Blake and his iconic "Always Be Closing" speech—which is not in the original play
Film cast included Al Pacino, Jack Lemmon, Ed Harris, Alan Arkin, and Kevin Spacey; the cast jokingly nicknamed it "Death of a F***in' Salesman"
Mamet based the play on his own experience working briefly at a Chicago real-estate office
The play's staccato, profanity-laden dialogue became Mamet's signature style, often called "Mamet-speak"
items.cardFront: David Mamet, 1984 — desperate real-estate salesmen, Chicago

items.prompt: Which 1955 Tennessee Williams play, set on a Mississippi Delta plantation during a birthday celebration, explores a former football hero's alcoholism, his wife Maggie's frustrated desires, and a dying patriarch called Big Daddy?
items.answer: Cat on a Hot Tin Roof
items.explanation:
Opened March 24, 1955 at the Morosco Theatre; directed by Elia Kazan, starring Ben Gazzara (Brick), Barbara Bel Geddes (Maggie), and Burl Ives (Big Daddy)
Won the 1955 Pulitzer Prize for Drama—Williams's second Pulitzer, after *A Streetcar Named Desire*
Original run of 694 performances; lost the Tony for Best Play to *The Diary of Anne Frank*
Kazan persuaded Williams to revise the third act to make Big Daddy return; Williams published both versions, disagreeing with the change
The 1958 film starred Elizabeth Taylor and Paul Newman; the Production Code forced removal of the play's homosexual subtext
Brick's drinking and refusal to sleep with Maggie stem from guilt over the death of his friend Skipper—the nature of their relationship is the play's central ambiguity
The title metaphor (Maggie compares herself to a cat on a hot tin roof) captures her desperate, clinging determination to hold on
items.cardFront: Tennessee Williams, 1955 — Maggie, Brick, Big Daddy, Mississippi plantation
items.cardBack: Cat on a Hot Tin Roof

items.prompt: Which 1895 Oscar Wilde comedy of manners follows two Victorian gentlemen who each invent fictional alter egos to escape social obligations, only to discover that being christened with a particular name is essential for marriage?
items.explanation:
Premiered February 14, 1895 at the St James's Theatre in London; produced by actor-manager George Alexander
Subtitled "A Trivial Comedy for Serious People"—the inversion is deliberate, as it satirizes every Victorian value it touches
Originally written in four acts; cut to three at the theatre's request
Ran only 86 performances; Wilde's arrest for "gross indecency" (homosexuality) in April 1895 forced its closure
The pun in the title—"earnest" meaning both the name Ernest and the quality of sincerity—drives the entire plot
Wilde's last play; he was sentenced to two years' hard labor and never wrote for the stage again
Widely regarded as the greatest comedy of manners in the English language; its wit has kept it in near-continuous production for over 130 years
items.cardFront: Oscar Wilde, 1895 — Victorian comedy, fictional alter egos, name pun

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare tragedy, written circa 1600, follows a Danish prince feigning madness to avenge his father's murder by his uncle, and includes the soliloquy 'To be, or not to be'?
alternates: The Tragedy of Hamlet, Prince of Denmark
items.explanation:
Written c. 1599–1601; first performed by the Lord Chamberlain's Men at the Globe Theatre, with Richard Burbage as the Prince
At 4,042 lines, it is Shakespeare's longest play; an uncut performance runs over four hours
"To be, or not to be" is the most famous soliloquy in English literature; "the play's the thing" and "brevity is the soul of wit" also originate here
The play-within-a-play ("The Mousetrap") that Hamlet stages to catch King Claudius is one of theatre's earliest examples of metadrama
Based on a Scandinavian legend recorded by Saxo Grammaticus in the 12th century; an earlier version (the "Ur-Hamlet," possibly by Thomas Kyd) is now lost
It is the most-produced Shakespeare play and one of the most-performed works in world theatre; nearly every generation's leading actors take on the role
The phrase "shuffle off this mortal coil," "method in the madness," and "to thine own self be true" all come from this play
items.cardFront: Shakespeare, c. 1600 — Danish prince, revenge, 'To be, or not to be'
